content.problemStatement:
### Welcome to the captivating world of prime numbers! 
Primes are like mystical gems in the realm of mathematics. They are natural numbers greater than $1$, possessing a fascinating secret: they have only two positive divisors $1$ and the number itself. They are special because they cannot be divided by any other numbers.

# Some examples of the primes numbers include $2$, $3$, $5$, $7$, $11$, $13$, $17$ etc.

Some examples of non-prime numbers (also called composite numbers) are $4$ (since it has a divisor $2$ which is not $1$ or $4$), $12$ (since it has the divisors $2, 3, 4, 6$ which are not $1$ or $12$), $9$, etc.

An exception is the number $1$, which is defined to be neither a prime or a composite number.

Ever wondered **Why $2$ is the lone even prime in the universe of numbers**? Well the answer lies in the fact that every even number is of the form $2*n$ and they necessary have 2 as their factor which is other than 1 and the number itself, This pattern of $2$ being the only even prime number is the base of various algorithmic optimizations.

Prime numbers are valuable in programming as they provide efficient solutions to a wide range of problems, allowing programmers to optimize algorithms and tackle various challenges with elegance and effectiveness.

**Lets dive deep into the world of Primes**.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
